messages.content: You are FarmHealth AI, an offline agricultural and livestock-health advisor for farmers and extension officers in northern Nigeria. Give practical, region-appropriate advice. Answer in the language the user used.
My millet has pale yellow streaks on the leaves and some heads have turned into twisted leafy 'green ears' instead of grain. What is it?

That is downy mildew, the main disease of millet here - the pale streaked leaves and the malformed leafy 'green ear' in place of grain are the giveaways. It is carried on seed and survives in soil and crop debris, and spreads faster in cool, wet conditions. You cannot cure affected plants, so manage it for this season and the next: pull and destroy infected plants, including the green ears, before they shed spores; use clean certified or resistant seed; treat seed with a recommended dressing before planting; and rotate away from millet on badly affected land. Do not save seed from an infected field.

meta.subject: millet / disease
meta.source_reference: verify: ICRISAT millet downy mildew guide

messages.content: You are FarmHealth AI, an offline agricultural and livestock-health advisor for farmers and extension officers in northern Nigeria. Give practical, region-appropriate advice. Answer in the language the user used.
What spacing and plant population should I use for cowpea (beans)?

For a spreading cowpea, a common guide is rows about 60 to 75 cm apart with plants about 20 to 25 cm apart in the row, sowing 2 to 3 seeds per hole and thinning to the strongest 2. Erect, early types can go a little closer in the row for a denser stand. Drop the seed about 3 to 5 cm deep into moist soil. A good stand helps smother weeds and can slow aphid spread, but overcrowding raises humidity and disease, so do not plant too densely. Confirm the exact spacing for your specific variety with your seed source or extension officer, and adjust to your soil moisture.

You are FarmHealth AI, an offline agricultural and livestock-health advisor for farmers and extension officers in northern Nigeria. Give practical, region-appropriate advice. Answer in the language the user used.
My groundnut leaves have dark brown spots with yellow halos and the leaves are falling off early. What is wrong?

Those dark brown to black spots ringed with a yellow halo, followed by early leaf drop, are groundnut leaf spot (early and late leaf spot), the commonest foliar disease of groundnut and a major cause of yield loss through defoliation. It builds up in warm, humid conditions and survives on crop debris. Manage it with resistant or tolerant varieties where available, rotation away from groundnut for a season or two, clearing infected haulm away from new fields, and early planting so the crop escapes the worst late-season build-up. If pressure is high a recommended fungicide can be used strictly per the label, but resistant varieties and cultural control are the backbone.

meta.subject: groundnut / disease
meta.source_reference: verify: ICRISAT groundnut leaf spot guide

messages.content: You are FarmHealth AI, an offline agricultural and livestock-health advisor for farmers and extension officers in northern Nigeria. Give practical, region-appropriate advice. Answer in the language the user used.
How do I keep my goats well fed during the dry season when there is little grass?

Plan ahead from harvest, because dry-season feeding is won earlier. Conserve crop residues instead of burning them - groundnut and cowpea haulms are protein-rich and ideal, along with sorghum and millet stover and cured grass hay - and store them dry under cover. Through the dry months, offer those residues and supplement with a little concentrate or by-products such as bran to cover the protein gap, especially for pregnant or nursing does. Browse from fodder trees and shrubs is valuable where available. Always provide clean water and a salt or mineral lick, and introduce any new feed gradually so the rumen adjusts.

You are FarmHealth AI, an offline agricultural and livestock-health advisor for farmers and extension officers in northern Nigeria. Give practical, region-appropriate advice. Answer in the language the user used.
Yaya zan gina wa awakina sansani (housing) mai kyau?

Manufar ita ce a kare awaki daga ruwan sama, zafi, sanyi da kwari, kuma a kiyaye tsabta. Gina sansani a wuri mai tudu inda ruwa ba ya taruwa, da rufin da ke kare daga rana da ruwa. Yi shimfida (floor) da za a iya tsabtacewa cikin sauki, kuma ka tabbatar akwai iska mai shiga don rage danshi da cututtukan numfashi. Ka raba kananan 'yan awaki da manya idan ya cancanta, ka bar sarari isasshe kar su cika juna. Ka tsabtace sansanin akai-akai ka cire taki domin rage kwari da kamuwa da cuta, kuma ka tabbatar suna da ruwa mai tsabta da inuwa.

You are FarmHealth AI, an offline agricultural and livestock-health advisor for farmers and extension officers in northern Nigeria. Give practical, region-appropriate advice. Answer in the language the user used.
How should I fatten rams to sell at a good weight for Sallah?

Start about three to four months before Sallah and pick healthy young rams with a good frame. Deworm them at the start, because internal parasites quietly eat your feed and gains. Build the ration around good forage (haulms, hay, browse) plus a measured energy and protein supplement such as bran and oilseed cake, increasing the concentrate gradually rather than suddenly so you do not upset the rumen or cause bloat. Provide clean water at all times and a mineral lick, and keep them in a clean, shaded pen with limited movement so energy goes to weight. Watch body condition and sell a few days before Sallah when demand and prices peak.

You are FarmHealth AI, an offline agricultural and livestock-health advisor for farmers and extension officers in northern Nigeria. Give practical, region-appropriate advice. Answer in the language the user used.
My local dairy cow's milk yield is dropping. How should I feed her to support production?

Milk yield follows nutrition, water and body condition, so work through each. A milking cow needs energy and protein above maintenance: base the ration on good forage (fresh grass or cured hay, legume haulms) and add a measured concentrate or by-products such as bran and cottonseed cake according to her yield and stage of lactation, increasing gradually. Water is the cheapest milk booster - a milking cow drinks a great deal, so clean water must always be available or yield falls fast. Provide a salt and mineral lick and do not let her lose condition in early lactation. Rule out other causes of a sudden drop such as heat stress, mastitis, being on heat, or disease; if feeding and water are right but yield keeps falling, have an animal-health officer check her. Match feed to her actual yield rather than overfeeding a low-yielder.

You are FarmHealth AI, an offline agricultural and livestock-health advisor for farmers and extension officers in northern Nigeria. Give practical, region-appropriate advice. Answer in the language the user used.
My catfish pond water has turned cloudy and the fish keep coming to the surface to gulp air. What is wrong?

Fish gulping at the surface usually means low dissolved oxygen and rising waste (ammonia), and cloudy, smelly water points the same way - most often from overfeeding and overcrowding. Act now: stop or cut feeding for a day, and change or top up a good portion of the water with clean water to dilute the waste. Going forward, feed only what the fish finish in a few minutes, because uneaten feed rots and fouls the water, and do not overstock for the water you can change. Provide aeration if you can, even splashing fresh water in helps, and remove any dead fish immediately. Clean, well-oxygenated water is the foundation of catfish health.

You are FarmHealth AI, an offline agricultural and livestock-health advisor for farmers and extension officers in northern Nigeria. Give practical, region-appropriate advice. Answer in the language the user used.
Some of my chickens have wart-like scabby bumps on their combs, wattles and around the eyes. What is it?

Those raised, wart-like scabs on the unfeathered skin - comb, wattles and around the eyes - are the dry form of fowl pox, a viral disease spread by biting insects (especially mosquitoes) and through skin wounds. It usually spreads slowly and many birds recover, but it sets back growth and laying, and a wet form affecting the mouth and throat is more serious. There is no cure for the virus, so manage it: isolate affected birds, keep wounds clean, control mosquitoes and reduce standing water, and support birds with good feed and clean water. The real protection where fowl pox is common is vaccination - discuss a plan with your animal-health officer, and confirm the diagnosis if you are unsure.

You are FarmHealth AI, an offline agricultural and livestock-health advisor for farmers and extension officers in northern Nigeria. Give practical, region-appropriate advice. Answer in the language the user used.
My grower birds are dying with yellow-green droppings, pale combs and they look weak and feverish. What could this be?

Weakness, fever, pale or shrunken combs and sulphur-yellow to greenish droppings in growers and adults is a classic picture of fowl typhoid, a bacterial disease (Salmonella Gallinarum) that can cause steady mortality. It spreads through droppings, contaminated feed and water, and can pass through the egg from infected hens, so it tends to recur on a farm once established. Because it overlaps with other diseases, isolate affected birds, tighten hygiene of feeders and drinkers, control rodents, and dispose of dead birds properly. This is a bacterial disease that needs veterinary diagnosis, ideally laboratory confirmation, because treatment and the decision about carrier birds should be guided by a vet rather than guessed. Prevention rests on clean stock, hygiene, and not mixing in birds of unknown status.

You are FarmHealth AI, an offline agricultural and livestock-health advisor for farmers and extension officers in northern Nigeria. Give practical, region-appropriate advice. Answer in the language the user used.
Several of my goats have high fever, runny eyes and nose, sores in the mouth and diarrhoea, and some have died quickly. What is it?

Fever with heavy eye and nasal discharge, mouth sores, diarrhoea and rapid death across several goats strongly suggests PPR (peste des petits ruminants), a fast-spreading, deadly viral disease of goats and sheep. There is no cure once animals are sick, so act on the herd now: isolate sick animals immediately, stop animals moving on and off your farm, and contact an animal-health officer quickly. Provide clean water and soft feed, and treat secondary infection only as a vet advises. The real protection going forward is the PPR vaccine for your healthy stock, which is far cheaper than losing animals, plus quarantine of any newly bought goats and disinfection of pens and equipment.

You are FarmHealth AI, an offline agricultural and livestock-health advisor for farmers and extension officers in northern Nigeria. Give practical, region-appropriate advice. Answer in the language the user used.
Yaya zan kare awakina daga cutar PPR?

PPR cuta ce mai saurin yaduwa kuma mai kisa, kuma babu maganinta da zarar ta kama, don haka rigakafi shi ne kariya mafi muhimmanci. Ka yi wa awaki masu lafiya allurar rigakafin PPR bisa shawarar likitan dabbobi (yawanci sau daya a shekara). Idan ka sayo sababbin awaki, ka raba su (quarantine) na akalla mako biyu zuwa uku kafin ka hada su da sauran, domin kar ka shigo da cuta. Ka iyakance shigowa da fitar dabbobi lokacin barkewa a yankin, ka tsabtace ka disinfect sansani da kayan aiki, kuma ka ware duk dabbar da ta yi rashin lafiya nan da nan. Idan ka ga alamun cuta, ka tuntubi jami'in lafiyar dabbobi da gaggawa.

You are FarmHealth AI, an offline agricultural and livestock-health advisor for farmers and extension officers in northern Nigeria. Give practical, region-appropriate advice. Answer in the language the user used.
My cow's udder is hot, swollen and painful, and the milk from one quarter is watery with clots. What is going on?

A hot, swollen, painful quarter with abnormal milk - watery, clotted or bloody - is mastitis, an infection and inflammation of the udder, and what you describe is clinical mastitis. It needs prompt attention because it can spread and permanently damage the quarter. Isolate the cow and milk her last, and strip out the affected quarter often to remove infected milk, which must not be fed to people or calves. Improve milking hygiene at once: clean dry hands and teats, a clean milking area, and teat disinfection after milking. This is a case for a vet, because the right treatment depends on the type of infection and antibiotic and milk-withholding decisions must be vet-guided, not self-prescribed. Prevent recurrence with clean bedding, good milking hygiene, and managing chronic cases on veterinary advice.

You are FarmHealth AI, an offline agricultural and livestock-health advisor for farmers and extension officers in northern Nigeria. Give practical, region-appropriate advice. Answer in the language the user used.
Awakina suna da kaikayi sosai, fatar jiki ta yi kauri da ƙiraje, gashi yana zubewa. Mene ne?

Wannan tana kama da ƙazamar fata (mange) wadda ƙwayoyin mites ke haifarwa - kaikayi mai tsanani, fata mai kauri da ƙiraje, da zubar gashi (musamman a kunne, fuska da ƙafafu) su ne manyan alamomi. Yana yaduwa cikin sauri ta hanyar taɓuwa tsakanin dabbobi da kuma kayan aiki da aka raba. Ka ware dabbobin da ke da cutar daga sauran nan da nan don rage yaduwa, ka tsabtace ka kashe ƙwari a sansani da kayan aiki. Wannan na bukatar magani da likitan dabbobi zai rubuta (acaricide da ya dace), don haka ka tuntubi jami'in lafiyar dabbobi don samun ingantaccen magani da yadda za a yi amfani da shi - kar ka yi amfani da magani ba tare da shawara ba. Ka kuma inganta abinci, domin dabba mai ƙarfi tana jure cutar fata da kyau.

You are FarmHealth AI, an offline agricultural and livestock-health advisor for farmers and extension officers in northern Nigeria. Give practical, region-appropriate advice. Answer in the language the user used.
How do I manage worms in my goats and sheep without wasting money on dewormer?

Internal worms quietly cut growth, weaken animals and can kill young or pregnant stock, so manage them smartly rather than blindly. Watch for a rough coat, weight loss, pot belly, pale gums (a sign of barber's-pole worm) and weakness, especially in the rains when worm burdens rise. Deworm strategically - for example before the rains and at points your vet advises - rather than dosing constantly, which wastes money and breeds resistant worms. Reduce the challenge by not overgrazing the same wet pasture, rotating grazing where possible, and keeping young stock off the most contaminated ground. Use the correct dewormer at the correct weight as your animal-health officer advises, improve nutrition so animals cope better, and ask about checking dung samples so you deworm only when needed.

You are FarmHealth AI, an offline agricultural and livestock-health advisor for farmers and extension officers in northern Nigeria. Give practical, region-appropriate advice. Answer in the language the user used.
How do I know the rains have truly started so I don't plant too early and lose my seed?

The danger is a 'false start' - one or two early rains followed by a dry spell that kills young seedlings. Treat the season as established only after two or three good rains in a row that wet the soil to a reasonable depth, not a single shower. Check that the soil holds moisture a hand-span down rather than drying out the next hot day. Once the onset is stable, plant within the first couple of weeks to use the full season. If you are unsure or the early rains look erratic, wait for that steady pattern and keep an early-maturing variety ready as a fallback so a late start still gives a harvest. Local extension advice or a seasonal forecast for your area is worth checking before you commit your seed.

meta.subject: rainfall / onset
meta.source_reference: verify: NAERLS planting calendar / seasonal forecast

messages.content: You are FarmHealth AI, an offline agricultural and livestock-health advisor for farmers and extension officers in northern Nigeria. Give practical, region-appropriate advice. Answer in the language the user used.
My area in northern Nasarawa keeps getting shorter, more erratic rains. Should I keep growing maize or switch to something hardier?

It depends on how short and unreliable your rains have become, and it need not be all-or-nothing. Maize gives the best yield but is the least forgiving of dry spells, so on genuinely short, erratic rainfall it is the riskiest. Sorghum and especially millet are far more drought-hardy, tolerate poorer sandy soils and mature faster, making them safer staples when the season is unreliable, and they double as fodder. A sound strategy is to shift your main planting toward an early-maturing sorghum or millet while keeping some early-maturing maize on your better, moisture-retaining land, spreading the risk rather than betting on one crop. Whatever you choose, use improved early-maturing varieties, practise moisture conservation such as tied ridges and mulching, and do not plant on a single rain. Confirm variety choices with your ADP for your specific area.

You are FarmHealth AI, an offline agricultural and livestock-health advisor for farmers and extension officers in northern Nigeria. Give practical, region-appropriate advice. Answer in the language the user used.
Lokacin zafi mai tsanani, yaya zan kare dabbobina (kaji da awaki) daga zafi?

Zafi mai tsanani na iya rage girma, rage ƙwai da madara, har ma ya kashe dabbobi, don haka ka rage musu zafi. Ka tabbatar akwai inuwa mai isa da iska mai shiga a sansani, kuma kar ka cika wuri da dabbobi domin cunkoso na ƙara zafi. Ruwa mai tsabta da sanyi shi ne kariya mafi muhimmanci - ka ba su ruwa a koyaushe, ka ƙara wuraren sha lokacin zafi. Ga kaji, ka guji aiki mai wahala kamar kamawa ko jigila da tsakar rana mai zafi, ka yi su da safe ko yamma. Ga awaki, ka bar su su huta a inuwa da rana, su yi kiwo da safe da yamma. Idan dabba ta yi numfashi da baki a buɗe ko ta gaji sosai, ka sanyaya ta da ruwa ka kai ta inuwa nan da nan.

You are FarmHealth AI, an offline agricultural and livestock-health advisor for farmers and extension officers in northern Nigeria. Give practical, region-appropriate advice. Answer in the language the user used.
Heavy rains are flooding parts of my farm. What can I do to protect my crops?

Your goal is to get excess water off the root zone fast, because waterlogged roots suffocate and rot. Open or clear drainage channels to lead standing water away from the field, and make sure existing furrows actually drain rather than pond. On land that floods often, plant on ridges or raised beds next season so the crop sits above the waterline, and avoid the lowest spots for sensitive crops. After the water drains the crop is weak and prone to disease, so do not add heavy nitrogen onto saturated soil and watch for fungal problems. Move stored produce and inputs onto raised platforms and out of the wet. Longer term, plan field layout and drainage before the peak rains rather than during them.

You are FarmHealth AI, an offline agricultural and livestock-health advisor for farmers and extension officers in northern Nigeria. Give practical, region-appropriate advice. Answer in the language the user used.
How do I store my cowpea (beans) so bruchid weevils don't destroy it?

The pest is the bruchid weevil, and the cheapest reliable defence is hermetic storage with no chemicals. First dry the beans well in the sun until they are hard and crack rather than dent, which lowers the moisture enough for safe storage. Then store them in triple-layer hermetic (PICS) bags, tying each inner liner and the outer bag tightly one after another so no air gets in; this cuts off oxygen and the weevils inside die within a couple of weeks, keeping the grain clean for months. Keep the bags off the floor and out of the rain, and avoid opening them repeatedly. Store only clean, dry, undamaged grain, since damaged seed spoils fastest.

You are FarmHealth AI, an offline agricultural and livestock-health advisor for farmers and extension officers in northern Nigeria. Give practical, region-appropriate advice. Answer in the language the user used.
How do I dry my maize properly before storage so it doesn't go mouldy?

Proper drying is what protects stored grain from mould and aflatoxin, so do not rush it into storage. Dry the maize in the sun on a clean tarpaulin or a raised drying floor, not on bare ground, turning it so it dries evenly, until the grain is hard and cracks rather than dents when bitten. Dry shelled grain rather than whole cobs where you can, and bring it in if rain threatens so it does not reabsorb moisture. Grain that feels warm or smells musty is not ready. Once properly dry, store it hermetically and keep it off the floor and away from damp. If you have a moisture meter, aim for the safe storage level your extension officer recommends.

You are FarmHealth AI, an offline agricultural and livestock-health advisor for farmers and extension officers in northern Nigeria. Give practical, region-appropriate advice. Answer in the language the user used.
How do I know when my groundnuts are ready to harvest?

Timing matters because lifting too early gives immature, shrivelled nuts and too late means pods detach and stay in the soil. Check maturity by lifting a few sample plants and opening pods: mature pods show a darkened, veined inner shell and the seed coat has its full variety colour, and most pods on the plant have filled. Yellowing foliage is a supporting sign but not reliable on its own. Lift when the soil is slightly moist, not bone dry, so pods come up with the plant instead of breaking off and being lost. After lifting, dry the pods properly, because good drying is critical to avoid mould and aflatoxin. Confirm the maturity window for your specific variety, since early and late types differ.

You are FarmHealth AI, an offline agricultural and livestock-health advisor for farmers and extension officers in northern Nigeria. Give practical, region-appropriate advice. Answer in the language the user used.
What is the best way to apply fertilizer to my maize so it isn't wasted?

How and when you apply matters as much as how much, especially on sandy soils where nutrients leach. Place fertilizer in a band beside the planting row or as spots near the plants rather than scattering it broadcast, and keep it a few centimetres from the stem so it does not scorch the plant. Split nitrogen into two or more applications - some early and the rest as the crop grows - so the next rains do not wash it all below the roots before the plant uses it. Apply to moist soil, not bone-dry or flooded ground, and cover it lightly so less is lost. Base your plan on your soil and crop need where you can, follow the recommended rates from your extension officer rather than guessing, and combine organic manure with mineral fertilizer to improve the soil over time.

You are FarmHealth AI, an offline agricultural and livestock-health advisor for farmers and extension officers in northern Nigeria. Give practical, region-appropriate advice. Answer in the language the user used.
Yaya zan haɗa abincin kaji da kaina a gona don rage tsada?

Manufar ita ce a samar da abinci mai daidaiton sinadarai (makamashi, furotin, ma'adanai) ba tare da ɓata kuɗi ba. Haɗa tushen makamashi (kamar masara ko dawa da aka niƙa) da tushen furotin (kamar waken soya da aka soya da niƙa, ko kek na gyada ko auduga) da ƙari na ma'adanai da alli (bone meal ko limestone) da gishiri kaɗan. Adadin ya dogara da matakin kaji - kajin nama ƙanana suna bukatar furotin mai yawa fiye da manya. Ka niƙa ka gauraya da kyau domin kaji su sha duka, kuma ka guji sinadarai marasa kyau ko da suka ruɓa, musamman waɗanda ka ke zargin suna da aflatoxin. Ka tabbatar ruwa mai tsabta a koyaushe. Don samun ma'auni daidai na haɗawa, ka nemi shawarar jami'in koyar da kiwo, domin kuskuren ma'auni na rage girma da ƙwai.

You are FarmHealth AI, an offline agricultural and livestock-health advisor for farmers and extension officers in northern Nigeria. Give practical, region-appropriate advice. Answer in the language the user used.
How do I select and keep good seed from this harvest for planting next season?

Good seed starts in the field: select from healthy, vigorous, true-to-type plants that yielded well and were free of disease, rather than picking leftovers at the end. Take seed from well-filled, mature heads, cobs or pods, and avoid plants that were diseased, lodged or off-type. Dry the seed thoroughly and clean out chaff, broken and shrivelled grains, and any pest-damaged seed. Store it separately from grain meant for eating, in a dry, airtight container, ideally labelled and dated, and protect it from weevils with hermetic storage. Before planting next season, run a quick germination test on a sample so you know it will come up well, and remember that saved seed from hybrids will not perform like the parent - buy fresh certified seed for hybrids.
